what: Power and Sample Size Calculation for Non-Proportional Hazards and Beyond
title: Power and Sample Size Calculation for Non-Proportional Hazards and Beyond
text: goal lrstat calculate power sample size non-proportional hazards using weighted log-rank tests analytically using simulations time--event group-sequential trial. built-capabilities use error-spending functions can calcualte power, accrual duration, follow-duration, absolute accrual rates Fleming-Harrington’s class weighting functions.

title: Power and Sample Size Calculation for Non-Proportional Hazards and Beyond
text: basic example shows solve common problem:
code: library(lrstat) ## basic example code for a two-stage group sequential trial with interim ## analysis at 80% of total number of events using Lan-DeMets O'Brien-Fleming ## error-spending. The accrual has a ramp-up periof of 9 months before  ## reaching 26 patients per month. The survival distribution for the treatment ## group has a delay effect of 6 months and a hazard ratio 0.58 after the delay.  ## The annual dropout rate is 5%. The accrual duration is 22 months.  ## The follow-up duration is 18 months for the last randomized patients.  ## The FH(0,1) weighted log-rank test is used for power calculation.   lrpower(kMax = 2, informationRates = c(0.8, 1),         alpha = 0.025, typeAlphaSpending = "sfOF",         allocationRatioPlanned = 1, accrualTime = seq(0, 9),         accrualIntensity = c(26/9*seq(1, 9), 26),         piecewiseSurvivalTime = c(0, 6),         lambda1 = c(0.0533, 0.0309),         lambda2 = c(0.0533, 0.0533),         gamma1 = -log(1-0.05)/12,         gamma2 = -log(1-0.05)/12,          accrualDuration = 22,         followupTime = 18, fixedFollowup = FALSE,          rho1 = 0, rho2 = 1)

what: BOIN Decision Table for Dose-Finding Trials — BOINTable
title: BOIN Decision Table for Dose-Finding Trials — BOINTable
text: Generates decision table Bayesian Optimal Interval (BOIN) design, widely used approach dose-escalation trials guides dose-finding decisions based observed toxicity rates.

title: BOIN Decision Table for Dose-Finding Trials — BOINTable
code: BOINTable(   nMax = NA_integer_,   pT = 0.3,   phi1 = 0.6 * pT,   phi2 = 1.4 * pT,   a = 1,   b = 1,   pExcessTox = 0.95 )

title: BOIN Decision Table for Dose-Finding Trials — BOINTable
text: nMax maximum number subjects allowed dose cohort. pT target toxicity probability. Defaults 0.3. phi1 lower equivalence limit target toxicity probability. phi2 upper equivalence limit target toxicity probability. prior toxicity shape parameter Beta prior. b prior non-toxicity shape parameter Beta prior. pExcessTox threshold excessive toxicity. posterior probability true toxicity rate exceeds pT greater pExcessTox, current higher doses excluded use protect future participants. Defaults 0.95.

title: BOIN Decision Table for Dose-Finding Trials — BOINTable
text: S3 class BOINTable object following components: settings: input settings data frame following variables: nMax: maximum number subjects dose cohort. pT: target toxicity probability. phi1: lower equivalence limit target toxicity probability. phi2: upper equivalence limit target toxicity probability. lambda1: lower decision boundary observed toxicity probability. lambda2: upper decision boundary observed toxicity probability. : prior toxicity parameter beta prior. b: prior non-toxicity parameter beta prior. pExcessTox: threshold excessive toxicity. decisionDataFrame: data frame listing dose-finding decisions combination sample size (n) number observed toxicities (y): n: Cohort size. y: Number observed toxicities. decision: Recommended action: escalate, de-escalate, stay current dose. decisionMatrix: matrix version decision table showing recommended action based number toxicities possible cohort size.

title: BOIN Decision Table for Dose-Finding Trials — BOINTable
text: Liu, S., & Yuan, Y. (2015). Bayesian optimal interval designs phase clinical trials. Journal Royal Statistical Society: Series C (Applied Statistics), 64(3), 507-523.

what: Adaptive Design at an Interim Look — adaptDesign
title: Adaptive Design at an Interim Look — adaptDesign
text: Calculates conditional power specified incremental information, given interim results, parameter value, data-dependent changes error spending function, number spacing interim looks. Conversely, calculates incremental information required attain specified conditional power, given interim results, parameter value, data-dependent changes error spending function, number spacing interim looks.

title: Adaptive Design at an Interim Look — adaptDesign

title: Adaptive Design at an Interim Look — adaptDesign
text: betaNew type II error secondary trial. INew maximum information secondary trial. Either betaNew INew provided, must missing. L interim adaptation look primary trial. zL z-test statistic interim adaptation look primary trial. theta assumed parameter value. IMax maximum information primary trial. Must provided. kMax maximum number stages primary trial. informationRates information rates primary trial. efficacyStopping Indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed stage primary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. futilityStopping Indicators whether futility stopping allowed stage primary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. criticalValues upper boundaries z-test statistic scale efficacy stopping primary trial. missing, boundaries computed based specified alpha spending function. alpha significance level primary trial. Defaults 0.025. typeAlphaSpending type alpha spending primary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "user" user defined spending, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpending parameter value alpha spending primary trial. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". userAlphaSpending user-defined alpha spending primary trial. Represents cumulative alpha spent stage. futilityBounds lower boundaries z-test statistic scale futility stopping primary trial. Defaults rep(-8, kMax-1) left unspecified. futilityCP conditional power-based futility bounds primary trial. futilityTheta parameter value-based futility bounds primary trial. spendingTime error spending time primary trial. Defaults missing, case assumed informationRates. MullerSchafer Whether use Muller Schafer (2001) method trial adaptation. kNew number looks secondary trial. informationRatesNew spacing looks secondary trial. efficacyStoppingNew indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed look secondary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. futilityStoppingNew indicators whether futility stopping allowed look secondary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. typeAlphaSpendingNew type alpha spending secondary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpendingNew parameter value alpha spending secondary trial. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". futilityBoundsInt futility boundaries z statistic scale new stages integrated trial. futilityCPInt conditional power-based futility bounds new stages integrated trial. futilityThetaInt parameter value-based futility bounds new stages integrated trial. typeBetaSpendingNew type beta spending secondary trial. One following: "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "user" user defined spending, "none" early futility stopping. Defaults "none". parameterBetaSpendingNew parameter value beta spending secondary trial. Corresponds \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". userBetaSpendingNew user-defined cumulative beta spending. Represents cumulative beta spent stage secondary trial. spendingTimeNew error spending time secondary trial. Defaults missing, case assumed informationRatesNew. varianceRatio ratio variance H0 variance H1.

title: Adaptive Design at an Interim Look — adaptDesign
text: Kaifeng Lu, kaifenglu@gmail.com

title: Adaptive Design at an Interim Look — adaptDesign
code: # two-arm randomized clinical trial with a normally distributed endpoint # 90% power to detect mean difference of 15 with a standard deviation of 50 # Design the Stage I Trial with 3 looks and Lan-DeMets O'Brien-Fleming type # spending function delta <- 15 sigma <- 50  (des1 <- getDesignMeanDiff(   beta = 0.1, meanDiff = delta, stDev = sigma,   kMax = 3, alpha = 0.025, typeAlphaSpending = "sfOF" )) #>                                                                                    #> Group-sequential design with 3 stages for two-sample mean difference               #> Mean difference under H0: 0, mean difference under H1: 15, standard deviation: 50  #> Overall power: 0.9003, overall alpha (1-sided): 0.025                              #> Drift parameter: 3.262, inflation factor: 1.012                                    #> Maximum information: 0.05, expected under H1: 0.04, expected under H0: 0.05        #> Maximum # subjects: 473, expected under H1: 379.2, expected under H0: 472          #> Allocation ratio: 1                                                                #> Alpha spending: Lan-DeMets O'Brien-Fleming, beta spending: None                    #>                                                                                    #>                               Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3 #> Information rate              0.334   0.666   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z)         3.706   2.513   1.993   #> Cumulative rejection          0.0343  0.5596  0.9003  #> Cumulative alpha spent        0.0001  0.0060  0.0250  #> Number of subjects            158.0   315.0   473.0   #> Efficacy boundary (mean diff) 29.484  14.159  9.163   #> Efficacy boundary (p)         0.0001  0.0060  0.0231  #> Information                   0.02    0.03    0.05     s1 <- des1$byStageResults$informationRates b1 <- des1$byStageResults$efficacyBounds n <- des1$overallResults$numberOfSubjects  # Monitoring the Stage I Trial L <- 1 nL <- des1$byStageResults$numberOfSubjects[L] deltahat <- 8 sigmahat <- 55 sedeltahat <- sigmahat * sqrt( 4 / nL) zL <- deltahat / sedeltahat  # Making an Adaptive Change: Stage I to Stage II # revised clinically meaningful difference downward to 10 power the study # retain the standard deviation at the design stage # Muller & Schafer (2001) method to design the secondary trial # with 2 looks and Lan-DeMets Pocock type spending function # re-estimate sample size to reach 90% conditional power deltaNew <- 10  (des2 <- adaptDesign(   betaNew = 0.1, L = L, zL = zL, theta = deltaNew,   IMax = n / (4 * sigma^2), kMax = 3, informationRates = s1,   alpha = 0.025, typeAlphaSpending = "sfOF",   MullerSchafer = TRUE, kNew = 2, typeAlphaSpendingNew = "sfP" )) #>                                                       #> Primary trial:                                        #> Group-sequential design with 3 stages                 #> Max information: 0.05                                 #> Interim adaptation look: 1, z-statistic value: 0.914  #> theta: 10                                             #> Conditional type I error: 0.0378                      #> Conditional power: 0.496, predictive power: 0.3877    #> Muller & Schafer method for secondary trial: TRUE     #>                                                       #>                       Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3 #> Information rate      0.334   0.666   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z) 3.706   2.513   1.993   #> Information           0.02    0.03    0.05    #>                                                                   #> Secondary trial:                                                  #> Group-sequential design with 2 stages                             #> Maximum information: 0.1                                          #> Overall power: 0.9, overall significance level (1-sided): 0.0378  #>                                                                   #>                        Stage 1 Stage 2 #> Information rate       0.500   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z)  1.988   2.018   #> Cumulative rejection   0.6157  0.9000  #> Cumulative alpha spent 0.0234  0.0378  #> Information            0.05    0.10    #>                                                       #> Integrated trial:                                     #> Group-sequential design with 3 stages                 #> Maximum information: 0.12                             #> Interim adaptation look: 1, z-statistic value: 0.914  #>                                                       #>                       Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3 #> Information rate      0.132   0.566   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z) 3.706   2.182   2.212   #> Information           0.02    0.07    0.12     INew <- des2$maxInformation (nNew <- ceiling(INew * 4 * sigma^2)) #> numeric(0) (nTotal <- nL + nNew) #> numeric(0)

title: Adaptive Multi-Arm Multi-Stage Design — adaptDesign_mams
text: Ping Gao, Yingqiu Li. Adaptive multiple comparison sequential design (AMCSD) clinical trials. Journal Biopharmaceutical Statistics, 2024, 34(3), 424-440.

title: Adaptive Multi-Arm Multi-Stage Design — adaptDesign_mams
text: Kaifeng Lu, kaifenglu@gmail.com

title: Adaptive Multi-Arm Multi-Stage Design — adaptDesign_mams
code: # Two active treatment arms are compared with a common control in a # two-look time-to-event design using O'Brien–Fleming–type alpha spending. # Suppose each active arm has a true hazard ratio of 0.75 versus control, # and the total number of events across all three arms at the final analysis # is 486. This corresponds to approximately 324 events for each active arm # versus the common control. Under these assumptions, the trial has about # 80% power to detect the treatment effect in at least one active arm.  (des1 <- getDesign_mams(   IMax = 324 / 4, theta = c(-log(0.75), -log(0.75)),   M = 2, r = 1, kMax = 2, informationRates = c(1/2, 1),   alpha = 0.025, typeAlphaSpending = "OF")) #>                                                                                 #> Multi-arm multi-stage design                                                    #> Overall power: 0.7994, overall alpha (1-sided): 0.025                           #> Number of active arms: 2                                                        #> Randomization ratio of each active vs. control: 1                               #> Using correlation for critical value calculation: TRUE                          #> Number of looks: 2                                                              #> Max information for pairwise comparion: 81                                      #> Max information for overall study: 121.5                                        #> Expected pairwise info under H1: 74.53, expected pairwise info under H0: 80.93  #> Expected overall info under H1: 111.8, expected overall info under H0: 121.4    #> Alpha spending: O'Brien-Fleming, beta spending: None                            #>                                                                                 #>                               Stage 1 Stage 2 #> Information rate              0.500   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z)         3.142   2.222   #> Cumulative rejection          0.1598  0.7994  #> Cumulative alpha spent        0.0016  0.0250  #> Efficacy boundary (theta)     0.494   0.247   #> Efficacy boundary (p)         0.0008  0.0132  #> Information for pairwise comp 40.50   81.00   #> Information for overall study 60.75   121.50  #>  #> By level critical values #>   Level Stage Boundary (Z) #> 1     2     1        3.142 #> 2     2     2        2.222 #> 3     1     1        2.797 #> 4     1     2        1.977  # Now assume that, at the interim analysis, the observed hazard ratios for # the two active arms versus control are 0.91 and 0.78, respectively. Using # the rule “drop any arm with an observed hazard ratio > 0.9”, arm 1 is # dropped. We then aim to achieve 80% conditional power to detect a hazard # ratio of 0.78 for the remaining arm at the final look. The analysis below # indicates that the required total number of events for arm 2 versus control # at the final analysis should be increased from 324 to 535. title: Adaptive Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — adaptDesign_seamless
text: Calculates conditional power specified incremental information, given interim results, parameter value, data-dependent changes error spending function, number spacing interim looks. Conversely, calculates incremental information required attain specified conditional power, given interim results, parameter value, data-dependent changes error spending function, number spacing interim looks.

title: Adaptive Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — adaptDesign_seamless
code: adaptDesign_seamless(   betaNew = NA_real_,   INew = NA_real_,   M = NA_integer_,   r = 1,   corr_known = TRUE,   L = NA_integer_,   zL = NA_real_,   theta = NA_real_,   IMax = NA_real_,   K = NA_integer_,   informationRates = NA_real_,   efficacyStopping = NA_integer_,   futilityStopping = NA_integer_,   criticalValues = NULL,   alpha = 0.025,   typeAlphaSpending = "sfOF",   parameterAlphaSpending = NA_real_,   userAlphaSpending = NA_real_,   futilityBounds = NULL,   futilityCP = NULL,   futilityTheta = NULL,   spendingTime = NA_real_,   MullerSchafer = FALSE,   kNew = NA_integer_,   informationRatesNew = NA_real_,   efficacyStoppingNew = NA_integer_,   futilityStoppingNew = NA_integer_,   typeAlphaSpendingNew = "sfOF",   parameterAlphaSpendingNew = NA_real_,   futilityBoundsInt = NULL,   futilityCPInt = NULL,   futilityThetaInt = NULL,   typeBetaSpendingNew = "none",   parameterBetaSpendingNew = NA_real_,   userBetaSpendingNew = NA_real_,   spendingTimeNew = NA_real_ )

title: Adaptive Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — adaptDesign_seamless
text: betaNew type II error secondary trial. INew maximum information active arm versus common control secondary trial. Either betaNew INew provided, must missing. M Number active treatment arms Phase 2. r Randomization ratio active arm common control Phase 2. corr_known Logical. TRUE, correlation Wald statistics Phase 2 derived randomization ratio \(r\) \(r / (r + 1)\). FALSE, conservative correlation 0 used. L interim adaptation look Phase 3. zL z-test statistic interim adaptation look Phase 3. theta assumed treatment effect selected arm versus common control. IMax Maximum information active arm versus common control original trial. Must provided. K Number sequential looks Phase 3. informationRates numeric vector information rates fixed trial. unspecified, defaults \((1:(K+1)) / (K+1)\). efficacyStopping Indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed stage primary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. futilityStopping Indicators whether futility stopping allowed stage primary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. criticalValues upper boundaries max z-test statistic scale Phase 2 z-test statistics selected arm Phase 3 primary trial. missing, boundaries computed based specified alpha spending function. alpha significance level primary trial. Defaults 0.025. typeAlphaSpending type alpha spending primary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "user" user defined spending, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpending parameter value alpha spending primary trial. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". userAlphaSpending user-defined alpha spending primary trial. Represents cumulative alpha spent stage. futilityBounds lower boundaries max-z statistic scale end phase 2 z-test statistic scale phase 3 futility stopping primary trial. Defaults rep(-8, kMax-1) left unspecified. futilityCP conditional power-based futility bounds primary trial. futilityTheta parameter value-based futility bounds primary trial. spendingTime error spending time primary trial. Defaults missing, case assumed informationRates. MullerSchafer Whether use Muller Schafer (2001) method trial adaptation. kNew number looks secondary trial. informationRatesNew spacing looks secondary trial. efficacyStoppingNew indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed look secondary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. futilityStoppingNew indicators whether futility stopping allowed look secondary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. typeAlphaSpendingNew type alpha spending secondary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpendingNew parameter value alpha spending secondary trial. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". futilityBoundsInt futility boundaries z statistic scale new stages integrated trial. futilityCPInt conditional power-based futility bounds new stages integrated trial. futilityThetaInt parameter value-based futility bounds new stages integrated trial. typeBetaSpendingNew type beta spending secondary trial. One following: "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "user" user defined spending, "none" early futility stopping. Defaults "none". parameterBetaSpendingNew parameter value beta spending secondary trial. Corresponds \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". userBetaSpendingNew user-defined cumulative beta spending. Represents cumulative beta spent stage secondary trial. spendingTimeNew error spending time secondary trial. Defaults missing, case assumed informationRatesNew.

what: Simulation for a Binary and a Time-to-Event Endpoint in Group Sequential Trials — binary_tte_sim
title: Simulation for a Binary and a Time-to-Event Endpoint in Group Sequential Trials — binary_tte_sim
text: Performs simulation two-endpoint two-arm group sequential trials. Endpoint 1: Binary endpoint, analyzed using Mantel-Haenszel test risk difference. Endpoint 2: Time--event endpoint, analyzed using log-rank test treatment effect. analysis times binary endpoint based calendar times, time--event analyses triggered reaching pre-specified number events. binary endpoint assessed first post-treatment follow-visit (PTFU1).

title: Simulation for a Binary and a Time-to-Event Endpoint in Group Sequential Trials — binary_tte_sim
code: binary_tte_sim(   kMax1 = 1,   kMax2 = 1,   riskDiffH0 = 0,   hazardRatioH0 = 1,   allocation1 = 1,   allocation2 = 1,   accrualTime = 0,   accrualIntensity = NA,   piecewiseSurvivalTime = 0,   stratumFraction = 1,   globalOddsRatio = NA,   pi1 = NA,   pi2 = NA,   lambda1 = NA,   lambda2 = NA,   gamma1 = NA,   gamma2 = NA,   delta1 = NA,   delta2 = NA,   upper1 = NA,   upper2 = NA,   n = NA,   plannedTime = NA,   plannedEvents = NA,   maxNumberOfIterations = 1000,   maxNumberOfRawDatasetsPerStage = 0,   seed = 0,   nthreads = 0 )

title: Simulation for a Binary and a Time-to-Event Endpoint in Group Sequential Trials — binary_tte_sim
text: kMax1 Number stages binary endpoint. kMax2 Number stages time--event endpoint. riskDiffH0 Risk difference null hypothesis binary endpoint. hazardRatioH0 Hazard ratio null hypothesis time--event endpoint. allocation1 Number subjects treatment group randomization block. Defaults 1 equal randomization. allocation2 Number subjects control group randomization block. Defaults 1 equal randomization. accrualTime vector specifies starting time piecewise Poisson enrollment time intervals. Must start 0, e.g., c(0, 3) breaks time axis 2 accrual intervals: \([0, 3)\) \([3, \infty)\). accrualIntensity vector accrual intensities. One accrual time interval. piecewiseSurvivalTime vector specifies starting time piecewise exponential survival time intervals. Must start 0, e.g., c(0, 6) breaks time axis 2 event intervals: \([0, 6)\) \([6, \infty)\). Defaults 0 exponential distribution. stratumFraction vector stratum fractions sum 1. Defaults 1 stratification. globalOddsRatio Global odds ratio Plackett copula linking two endpoints. pi1 Response probabilities stratum treatment group binary endpoint. pi2 Response probabilities stratum control group binary endpoint. lambda1 vector hazard rates event analysis time interval stratum treatment group time--event endpoint. lambda2 vector hazard rates event analysis time interval stratum control group time--event endpoint. gamma1 hazard rate exponential dropout, vector hazard rates piecewise exponential dropout applicable strata, vector hazard rates dropout analysis time interval stratum treatment group. gamma2 hazard rate exponential dropout, vector hazard rates piecewise exponential dropout applicable strata, vector hazard rates dropout analysis time interval stratum control group. delta1 hazard rate exponential treatment discontinuation, vector hazard rates piecewise exponential treatment discontinuation applicable strata, vector hazard rates treatment discontinuation analysis time interval stratum treatment group binary endpoint. delta2 hazard rate exponential treatment discontinuation, vector hazard rates piecewise exponential treatment discontinuation applicable strata, vector hazard rates treatment discontinuation analysis time interval stratum control group binary endpoint. upper1 Maximim protocol-specified treatment duration treatment group. upper2 Maximum protocol-specified treatment duration control group. n Sample size. plannedTime Calendar times analyses binary endpoint. plannedEvents Target cumulative number events time--event analyses. maxNumberOfIterations Number simulation iterations perform. maxNumberOfRawDatasetsPerStage Number subject-level datasets retain per stage. Set 0 skip raw data saving. seed seed reproduce simulation results. nthreads number threads use simulations (0 means default RcppParallel behavior).

stratum subject. treatmentGroup: treatment group (1 2) subject. survivalTime: underlying survival time time--event endpoint subject. dropoutTime: underlying dropout time time--event endpoint subject. One analysis time interval. gamma1 hazard rate exponential dropout, vector hazard rates piecewise exponential dropout active treatment group. gamma2 hazard rate exponential dropout, vector hazard rates piecewise exponential dropout control group. accrualDuration Duration enrollment period. maxFollowupTime Follow-time first enrolled subject. fixed follow-, maxFollowupTime = minFollowupTime. variable follow-, maxFollowupTime = accrualDuration + minFollowupTime.

title: Error Spending — errorSpent
text: t vector spending times, typically equal information fractions. error total error spend. sf spending function. One following: "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function. Defaults "sfOF". sfpar parameter spending function. Corresponds \(\rho\) "sfKD" \(\gamma\) "sfHSD".

title: Error Spending — errorSpent
text: vector errors spent interim look.

title: Error Spending — errorSpent
text: function implements variety error spending functions commonly used group sequential designs, assuming one-sided hypothesis testing. O'Brien-Fleming-Type Spending Function spending function allocates little alpha early alpha later trial. defined : $$ \alpha(t) = 2 - 2\Phi\left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{\sqrt{t}}\right), $$ \(\Phi\) standard normal cumulative distribution function, \(z_{\alpha/2}\) critical value standard normal distribution, \(t \[0, 1]\) denotes information fraction. Pocock-Type Spending Function function spends alpha evenly throughout study: $$ \alpha(t) = \alpha \log(1 + (e - 1)t), $$ \(e\) Euler's number (approximately 2.718). Kim DeMets Power-Type Spending Function family spending functions defined : $$ \alpha(t) = \alpha t^{\rho}, \quad \rho > 0. $$ \(\rho = 1\), function mimics Pocock-type boundaries. \(\rho = 3\), approximates O’Brien-Fleming-type boundaries. Hwang, Shih, DeCani Spending Function flexible family functions given : $$ \alpha(t) = \begin{cases} \alpha \frac{1 - e^{-\gamma t}}{1 - e^{-\gamma}}, & \text{} \gamma \ne 0 \\ \alpha t, & \text{} \gamma = 0. \end{cases} $$ \(\gamma = -4\), spending function resembles O’Brien-Fleming boundaries. \(\gamma = 1\), resembles Pocock boundaries.

title: Error Spending — errorSpent
text: Kaifeng Lu, kaifenglu@gmail.com

title: Error Spending — errorSpent

Defaults c(rep(-8.0, kMax-1), b[kMax]) left unspecified, kMax = length(b). theta Stagewise parameter interest, e.g., -U/V weighted log-rank test, U mean V variance weighted log-rank test score statistic stage. proportional hazards conventional log-rank test, use scalar input, theta = -log(HR). Defaults 0 corresponding null hypothesis. Stagewise cumulative information, e.g., V, variance weighted log-rank test score statistic stage. conventional log-rank test, information can approximated phi*(1-phi)*D, phi probability allocated active arm, D total number events stage. Defaults seq(1, kMax) left unspecified.

title: Exit Probabilities for Multi-Arm Multi-Stage Design — exitprob_mams
text: Ping Gao, Yingqiu Li. Adaptive multiple comparison sequential design (AMCSD) clinical trials. Journal Biopharmaceutical Statistics, 2024, 34(3), 424-440.

title: Exit Probabilities for Multi-Arm Multi-Stage Design — exitprob_mams
text: Kaifeng Lu, kaifenglu@gmail.com

title: Exit Probabilities for Multi-Arm Multi-Stage Design — exitprob_mams
code: # Setup: 2 active arms vs control and 3 sequential looks.  # Information levels: equal spacing over 3 looks based on a maximum of # 95 patients per arm, SD = 1.0 I <- 95 / (2 * 1.0^2) * seq(1, 3)/3  # O'Brien-Fleming critical values b <- c(3.886562, 2.748214, 2.243907)  # Type I error under the global null hypothesis p0 <- exitprob_mams(M = 2, theta = c(0, 0), kMax = 3, b = b, I = I) cumsum(p0$exitProbUpper) #> [1] 0.0001007465 0.0058081554 0.0250000399  # Power under alternative: Treatment effects of 0.3 and 0.5 p1 <- exitprob_mams(M = 2, theta = c(0.3, 0.5), kMax = 3, b = b, I = I) cumsum(p1$exitProbUpper) #> [1] 0.03130483 0.55104538 0.90221799

what: Exit Probabilities for Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — exitprob_seamless
title: Exit Probabilities for Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — exitprob_seamless
text: Computes upper lower exit probabilities phase 2/3 seamless design. Phase 2, multiple active arms compared common control arm. phase-2 max-Z statistic crosses efficacy boundary, trial stops early efficacy; falls futility boundary, trial stops early futility. Otherwise, best-performing arm selected proceed Phase 3, tested common control multiple looks upper optional lower stopping boundaries.

title: Exit Probabilities for Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — exitprob_seamless
code: exitprob_seamless(   M = NA_integer_,   r = 1,   theta = NA_real_,   corr_known = TRUE,   K = NA_integer_,   b = NULL,   a = NULL,   I = NULL )

title: Exit Probabilities for Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — exitprob_seamless
text: M Number active treatment arms Phase 2. r Randomization ratio active arm common control Phase 2. theta vector length \(M\) representing true treatment effects active arm versus common control. corr_known Logical. TRUE, correlation Wald statistics Phase 2 derived randomization ratio \(r\) \(r / (r + 1)\). FALSE, conservative correlation 0 used. K Number sequential looks Phase 3. b vector efficacy boundaries (length \(K+1\)). first element efficacy boundary phase-2 max-Z statistic; remaining \(K\) elements efficacy boundaries selected arm Phase 3. optional vector futility boundaries (length \(K+1\)). first element futility boundary phase-2 max-Z statistic; remaining \(K\) elements futility boundaries selected arm Phase 3. omitted, futility stopping applied. vector information levels (length \(K+1\)) active arm versus common control. first element Phase 2; remaining \(K\) elements looks Phase 3.

title: Exit Probabilities for Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — exitprob_seamless
text: list containing following components: exitProbUpper: vector length \(K + 1\). first element probability stopping efficacy Phase 2; remaining elements probabilities stopping efficacy look Phase 3. exitProbLower: vector length \(K + 1\). first element probability stopping futility Phase 2; remaining elements probabilities stopping futility look Phase 3. exitProbByArmUpper: \((K+1) \times M\) matrix. \((k, m)\)-th entry gives probability stopping efficacy look \(k\) given arm \(m\) selected best. exitProbByArmLower: \((K+1) \times M\) matrix. \((k, m)\)-th entry gives probability stopping futility look \(k\) given arm \(m\) selected best. selectAsBest: vector length \(M\) containing probability active arm selected move Phase 3. backward compatibility, list also contains: exitProb: identical exitProbUpper. exitProbByArm: identical exitProbByArmUpper.

title: Exit Probabilities for Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — exitprob_seamless
text: function assumes multivariate normal distribution Wald statistics. "best" arm defined active arm largest Z-statistic end Phase 2 among designs continue beyond phase-2 analysis. Decision Rules: Phase 2 efficacy stop: reject phase-2 max-Z statistic satisfies \(\max_m Z_m(I_0) \ge b_0\). Phase 2 futility stop: stop futility phase-2 max-Z statistic satisfies \(\max_m Z_m(I_0) \le a_0\). Continue Phase 3: \(a_0 < \max_m Z_m(I_0) < b_0\), select arm largest phase-2 Z-statistic continue arm . Phase 3 efficacy stop: look \(k\), reject selected arm's Z-statistic exceeds efficacy boundary earlier stop occurred. Phase 3 futility stop: look \(k\), stop futility selected arm's Z-statistic futility boundary earlier stop occurred. Design Assumptions: active arms share information level Phase 2. Exactly one active arm selected end Phase 2 based largest observed Z-statistic trial continues Phase 3.

title: Exit Probabilities for Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — exitprob_seamless
text: Ping Gao, Yingqiu Li. Adaptive two-stage seamless sequential design clinical trials. Journal Biopharmaceutical Statistics, 2025, 35(4), 565-587.

title: Exit Probabilities for Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — exitprob_seamless
text: Kaifeng Lu, kaifenglu@gmail.com

title: Exit Probabilities for Phase 2/3 Seamless Design — exitprob_seamless
code: # Setup: 2 active arms vs control in Phase 2; 1 selected arm vs control # in Phase 3. Phase 3 has 2 sequential looks.  # Information levels: equal spacing over 3 looks based on a maximum of # 110 patients per arm, SD = 1.0 I <- c(110 / (2 * 1.0^2) * seq(1, 3)/3)  # O'Brien-Fleming efficacy boundaries b <- c(3.776605, 2.670463, 2.180424)  # No futility stopping p0 <- exitprob_seamless(M = 2, theta = c(0, 0), K = 2, b = b, I = I) cumsum(p0$exitProbUpper) #> [1] 0.0001572756 0.0066431322 0.0250000060  # Add futility stopping a <- c(0, 0.5, b[3]) p1 <- exitprob_seamless(M = 2, theta = c(0.3, 0.5), K = 2, b = b, a = a, I = I) cbind(   cumulativeEfficacy = cumsum(p1$exitProbUpper),   cumulativeFutility = cumsum(p1$exitProbLower) ) #>      cumulativeEfficacy cumulativeFutility #> [1,]         0.05477567        0.007803144 #> [2,]         0.62292767        0.014040545 #> [3,]         0.89800885        0.101991188

IMax maximum information primary trial. kMax maximum number stages primary trial. informationRates information rates primary trial. efficacyStopping Indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed stage primary trial. Defaults true left unspecified. criticalValues upper boundaries z-test statistic scale efficacy stopping primary trial. alpha significance level primary trial. Defaults 0.025. typeAlphaSpending type alpha spending primary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpending parameter value alpha spending primary trial. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". spendingTime error spending time primary trial. Defaults missing, case, informationRates. MullerSchafer Whether use Muller Schafer (2001) method trial adaptation. Lc termination look integrated trial. zLc z-test statistic termination look integrated trial. INew maximum information secondary trial. informationRatesNew spacing looks secondary trial look L2. efficacyStoppingNew indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed look secondary trial look L2. Defaults true left unspecified. typeAlphaSpendingNew type alpha spending secondary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpendingNew parameter value alpha spending secondary trial. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". spendingTimeNew error spending time secondary trial look L2. Defaults missing, case, informationRatesNew.

title: Confidence Interval After Adaptation — getADCI
text: Kaifeng Lu, kaifenglu@gmail.com

title: Confidence Interval After Adaptation — getADCI
code: # two-arm randomized clinical trial with a normally distributed endpoint # 90% power to detect mean difference of 15 with a standard deviation of 50 # Design the Stage I Trial with 3 looks and Lan-DeMets O'Brien-Fleming type # spending function delta <- 15 sigma <- 50  (des1 <- getDesignMeanDiff(   beta = 0.1, meanDiff = delta, stDev = sigma,   kMax = 3, alpha = 0.025, typeAlphaSpending = "sfOF" )) #>                                                                                    #> Group-sequential design with 3 stages for two-sample mean difference               #> Mean difference under H0: 0, mean difference under H1: 15, standard deviation: 50  #> Overall power: 0.9003, overall alpha (1-sided): 0.025                              #> Drift parameter: 3.262, inflation factor: 1.012                                    #> Maximum information: 0.05, expected under H1: 0.04, expected under H0: 0.05        #> Maximum # subjects: 473, expected under H1: 379.2, expected under H0: 472          #> Allocation ratio: 1                                                                #> Alpha spending: Lan-DeMets O'Brien-Fleming, beta spending: None                    #>                                                                                    #>                               Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3 #> Information rate              0.334   0.666   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z)         3.706   2.513   1.993   #> Cumulative rejection          0.0343  0.5596  0.9003  #> Cumulative alpha spent        0.0001  0.0060  0.0250  #> Number of subjects            158.0   315.0   473.0   #> Efficacy boundary (mean diff) 29.484  14.159  9.163   #> Efficacy boundary (p)         0.0001  0.0060  0.0231  #> Information                   0.02    0.03    0.05     s1 <- des1$byStageResults$informationRates b1 <- des1$byStageResults$efficacyBounds n <- des1$overallResults$numberOfSubjects  # Monitoring the Stage I Trial L <- 1 nL <- des1$byStageResults$numberOfSubjects[L] deltahat <- 8 sigmahat <- 55 sedeltahat <- sigmahat * sqrt( 4 / nL) zL <- deltahat / sedeltahat  # Making an Adaptive Change: Stage I to Stage II # revised clinically meaningful difference downward to 10 power the study # retain the standard deviation at the design stage # Muller & Schafer (2001) method to design the secondary trial # with 2 looks and Lan-DeMets Pocock type spending function # re-estimate sample size to reach 90% conditional power deltaNew <- 10  (des2 <- adaptDesign(   betaNew = 0.1, L = L, zL = zL, theta = deltaNew,   IMax = n / (4 * sigma^2), kMax = 3, informationRates = s1,   alpha = 0.025, typeAlphaSpending = "sfOF",   MullerSchafer = TRUE, kNew = 2, typeAlphaSpendingNew = "sfP" )) #>                                                       #> Primary trial:                                        #> Group-sequential design with 3 stages                 #> Max information: 0.05                                 #> Interim adaptation look: 1, z-statistic value: 0.914  #> theta: 10                                             #> Conditional type I error: 0.0378                      #> Conditional power: 0.496, predictive power: 0.3877    #> Muller & Schafer method for secondary trial: TRUE     #>                                                       #>                       Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3 #> Information rate      0.334   0.666   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z) 3.706   2.513   1.993   #> Information           0.02    0.03    0.05    #>                                                                   #> Secondary trial:                                                  #> Group-sequential design with 2 stages                             #> Maximum information: 0.1                                          #> Overall power: 0.9, overall significance level (1-sided): 0.0378  #>                                                                   #>                        Stage 1 Stage 2 #> Information rate       0.500   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z)  1.988   2.018   #> Cumulative rejection   0.6157  0.9000  #> Cumulative alpha spent 0.0234  0.0378  #> Information            0.05    0.10    #>                                                       #> Integrated trial:                                     #> Group-sequential design with 3 stages                 #> Maximum information: 0.12                             #> Interim adaptation look: 1, z-statistic value: 0.914  #>                                                       #>                       Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3 #> Information rate      0.132   0.566   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z) 3.706   2.182   2.212   #> Information           0.02    0.07    0.12     INew <- des2$secondaryTrial$maxInformation (nNew <- ceiling(INew * 4 * sigma^2)) #> [1] 1042 (nTotal <- nL + nNew) #> [1] 1200  # Monitoring the Integrated Trial s2 <- des2$secondaryTrial$informationRates  Lc <- 2 deltahatc <- 9.5 sigmahatc <- 52.759 L2 <- Lc - L nL2 <-  nNew * s2[L2] nc <- nL + nL2 sedeltahatc <- sigmahatc * sqrt(4 / nc) zLc <- deltahatc / sedeltahatc zL2 <- (zLc * sqrt(nc) - zL * sqrt(nL)) / sqrt(nL2)  getADCI(   L = L, zL = zL, IMax = n / (4 * sigmahatc^2), kMax = 3,   informationRates = s1, alpha = 0.025, typeAlphaSpending = "sfOF",   MullerSchafer = TRUE, Lc = Lc, zLc = zLc,   INew = nNew / (4 * sigmahatc^2), informationRatesNew = s2,   typeAlphaSpendingNew = "sfP") #>       pvalue thetahat cilevel    lower    upper #> 1 0.01237657 9.349566    0.95 1.216479 17.36533

code: getADCI_mams(   M = NA_integer_,   r = 1,   corr_known = TRUE,   L = NA_integer_,   zL = NA_real_,   IMax = NA_real_,   kMax = NA_integer_,   informationRates = NA_real_,   efficacyStopping = NA_integer_,   criticalValues = NULL,   alpha = 0.25,   typeAlphaSpending = "sfOF",   parameterAlphaSpending = NA_real_,   spendingTime = NA_real_,   MullerSchafer = FALSE,   MNew = NA_integer_,   selected = NA_integer_,   rNew = 1,   Lc = NA_integer_,   zLc = NA_real_,   INew = NA_real_,   informationRatesNew = NA_real_,   efficacyStoppingNew = NA_integer_,   typeAlphaSpendingNew = "sfOF",   parameterAlphaSpendingNew = NA_real_,   spendingTimeNew = NA_real_ )

title: Confidence Interval After Adaptation for Multi-Arm Multi-Stage Design — getADCI_mams
text: M Number active treatment arms primary trial. r Randomization ratio active arm common control primary trial. corr_known Logical. TRUE, correlation Wald statistics derived randomization ratio \(r\) \(r / (r + 1)\). FALSE, conservative correlation 0 assumed. L interim adaptation look primary trial. zL z-test statistics interim adaptation look primary trial. IMax Maximum information active arm versus common control primary trial. Must provided. kMax maximum number stages primary trial. informationRates information rates primary trial. efficacyStopping Indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed stage primary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. criticalValues matrix -level upper boundaries max z-test statistic scale efficacy stopping look L primary trial. first column level M, second column level M - 1, , last column level 1. left unspecified, critical values computed based specified alpha spending function. alpha significance level primary trial. Defaults 0.025. typeAlphaSpending type alpha spending primary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpending parameter value alpha spending primary trial. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". spendingTime error spending time primary trial. Defaults missing, case, informationRates. MullerSchafer Whether use Muller Schafer (2001) method trial adaptation. MNew number active treatment arms secondary trial. selected indices selected treatment arms secondary trial among M active arms primary trial. rNew randomization ratio active arm common control secondary trial. Lc termination look integrated trial. zLc z-test statistics termination look integrated trial. INew maximum information active arm versus common control secondary trial. informationRatesNew spacing looks secondary trial. efficacyStoppingNew indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed look secondary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. typeAlphaSpendingNew type alpha spending secondary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpendingNew parameter value alpha spending secondary trial. Corresponds \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". spendingTimeNew error spending time secondary trial. Defaults missing, case, informationRatesNew.

TRUE, correlation Wald statistics Phase 2 derived randomization ratio \(r\) \(r / (r + 1)\). FALSE, conservative correlation 0 used. L interim adaptation look Phase 3. zL z-test statistic interim adaptation look Phase 3. IMax Maximum information active arm versus common control original trial. Must provided. K Number sequential looks Phase 3. informationRates numeric vector information rates fixed trial. unspecified, defaults \((1:(K+1)) / (K+1)\). efficacyStopping Indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed stage primary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. criticalValues upper boundaries max z-test statistic scale Phase 2 z-test statistics selected arm Phase 3 primary trial. missing, boundaries computed based specified alpha spending function. alpha significance level primary trial. Defaults 0.025. typeAlphaSpending type alpha spending primary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpending parameter value alpha spending primary trial. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". spendingTime error spending time primary trial. Defaults missing, case, informationRates. MullerSchafer Whether use Muller Schafer (2001) method trial adaptation. Lc termination look integrated trial. zLc z-test statistic termination look integrated trial. INew maximum information active arm versus common control secondary trial. informationRatesNew spacing looks secondary trial. efficacyStoppingNew indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed look secondary trial. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. typeAlphaSpendingNew type alpha spending secondary trial. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpendingNew parameter value alpha spending secondary trial. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". spendingTimeNew error spending time secondary trial. Defaults missing, case, informationRatesNew.

title: Efficacy Boundaries for Group Sequential Design — getBound
text: k Look number current analysis. informationRates Information rates current look. Must increasing less equal 1. alpha significance level. Defaults 0.025. typeAlphaSpending type alpha spending. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "user" user defined spending, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpending parameter value alpha spending. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". userAlphaSpending user defined alpha spending. Cumulative alpha spent stage. spendingTime vector length k error spending time analysis. Must increasing less equal 1. Defaults missing, case, informationRates. efficacyStopping Indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed stage. Defaults TRUE left unspecified.

text: M Number active treatment arms. r Randomization ratio active arm common control. corr_known Logical. TRUE, correlation Wald statistics derived randomization ratio \(r\) \(r / (r + 1)\). Defaults 0.025. typeAlphaSpending type alpha spending. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "user" user defined spending, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpending parameter value alpha spending. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". userAlphaSpending user defined alpha spending. Cumulative alpha spent stage. spendingTime numeric vector length \(k\) specifying error spending time analysis. Values must strictly increasing \(\le 1\). omitted, defaults informationRates. efficacyStopping Indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed stage. Defaults TRUE left unspecified.

title: Confidence Interval After Trial Termination — getCI
code: # group sequential design with 90% power to detect delta = 6 delta <- 6 sigma <- 17 n <- 282 (des1 <- getDesign(IMax = n/(4*sigma^2), theta = delta, kMax = 3,                    alpha = 0.05, typeAlphaSpending = "sfHSD",                    parameterAlphaSpending = -4)) #>                                                                           #> Group-sequential design with 3 stages                                     #> theta: 6, maximum information: 0.24                                       #> Overall power: 0.9029, overall alpha (1-sided): 0.05                      #> Drift parameter: 2.963, inflation factor: 1.014                           #> Expected information under H1: 0.19, expected information under H0: 0.24  #> Alpha spending: HSD(gamma = -4), beta spending: None                      #>                                                                           #>                           Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3 #> Information rate          0.333   0.667   1.000   #> Efficacy boundary (Z)     2.794   2.289   1.680   #> Cumulative rejection      0.1395  0.5588  0.9029  #> Cumulative alpha spent    0.0026  0.0125  0.0500  #> Efficacy boundary (theta) 9.797   5.676   3.401   #> Efficacy boundary (p)     0.0026  0.0110  0.0465  #> Information               0.08    0.16    0.24     # crossed the boundary at the second look L <- 2 n1 <- n*2/3 delta1 <- 7 sigma1 <- 20 zL <- delta1/sqrt(4/n1*sigma1^2)  # confidence interval getCI(L = L, zL = zL, IMax = n/(4*sigma1^2),       informationRates = c(1/3, 2/3), alpha = 0.05,       typeAlphaSpending = "sfHSD", parameterAlphaSpending = -4) #>        pvalue thetahat cilevel    lower    upper #> 1 0.009815172 6.931527     0.9 2.069564 11.75432

title: Conditional Power for Generic Group Sequential Design — getCP

title: Conditional Power for Generic Group Sequential Design — getCP
text: Cyrus R. Mehta Stuart J. Pocock. Adaptive increase sample size interim results promising: practical guide examples. Stat Med. 2011;30:3267–3284.

title: Conditional Power for Generic Group Sequential Design — getCP
text: Kaifeng Lu, kaifenglu@gmail.com

title: Conditional Power for Generic Group Sequential Design — getCP
code: # Conditional power calculation with delayed treatment effect  # Two interim analyses have occurred with 179 and 266 events, # respectively. The observed hazard ratio at the second interim # look is 0.81.  trialsdt <- as.Date("2020-03-04")                       # trial start date iadt <- c(as.Date("2022-02-01"), as.Date("2022-11-01")) # interim dates mo1 <- as.numeric(iadt - trialsdt + 1)/30.4375          # interim months  # Assume a piecewise Poisson enrollment process with a 8-month ramp-up # and 521 patients were enrolled after 17.94 months N <- 521                   # total number of patients Ta <- 17.94                # enrollment duration Ta1 <- 8                   # assumed end of enrollment ramp-up enrate <- N / (Ta - Ta1/2) # enrollment rate after ramp-up  # Assume a median survival of 16.7 months for the control group, a # 5-month delay in treatment effect, and a hazard ratio of 0.7 after # the delay lam1 <- log(2)/16.7  # control group hazard of exponential distribution t1 <- 5              # months of delay in treatment effect hr <- 0.7            # hazard ratio after delay lam2 <- hr*lam1      # treatment group hazard after delay  # Assume an annual dropout rate of 5% gam <- -log(1-0.05)/12  # hazard for dropout  # The original target number of events was 298 and the new target is 335 mo2 <- caltime(   nevents = c(298, 335),   allocationRatioPlanned = 1,   accrualTime = seq(0, Ta1),   accrualIntensity = enrate*seq(1, Ta1+1)/(Ta1+1),   piecewiseSurvivalTime = c(0, t1),   lambda1 = c(lam1, lam2),   lambda2 = c(lam1, lam1),   gamma1 = gam,   gamma2 = gam,   accrualDuration = Ta,   followupTime = 1000)  # expected number of events and average hazard ratios (lr1 <- lrstat(   time = c(mo1, mo2),   accrualTime = seq(0, Ta1),   accrualIntensity = enrate*seq(1, Ta1+1)/(Ta1+1),   piecewiseSurvivalTime = c(0, t1),   lambda1 = c(lam1, lam2),   lambda2 = c(lam1, lam1),   gamma1 = gam,   gamma2 = gam,   accrualDuration = Ta,   followupTime = 1000,   predictTarget = 3)) #>       time subjects  nevents  nevents1  nevents2 ndropouts ndropouts1 #> 1 22.99795      521 184.4200  85.65447  98.76552  20.64012   10.46884 #> 2 31.96715      521 267.7201 122.68031 145.03981  30.85289   15.91609 #> 3 36.23725      521 298.0000 136.77727 161.22273  34.59342   17.99003 #> 4 42.68854      521 335.0000 154.60965 180.39035  39.19088   20.61354 #>   ndropouts2 nfmax nfmax1 nfmax2     uscore   vscore  logRankZ hazardRatioH0 #> 1   10.17128     0      0      0  -7.773866 46.07799 -1.145223             1 #> 2   14.93680     0      0      0 -15.171213 66.79076 -1.856360             1 #> 3   16.60338     0      0      0 -17.860967 74.26549 -2.072581             1 #> 4   18.57735     0      0      0 -21.139606 83.32376 -2.315861             1 #>          HR     vlogHR    zlogHR #> 1 0.8445734 0.02180878 -1.143865 #> 2 0.7966399 0.01506393 -1.852382 #> 3 0.7861833 0.01353711 -2.067617 #> 4 0.7760959 0.01204385 -2.309722   hr2 <- 0.81                    # observed hazard ratio at interim 2 z2 <- (-log(hr2))*sqrt(266/4)  # corresponding z-test statistic value  # expected mean of -log(HR) at the original looks and the new final look theta <- -log(lr1$HR[c(1,2,3,4)])  # conditional power with sample size increase getCP(INew = (335 - 266)/4,       L = 2, zL = z2, theta = theta,       IMax = 298/4, kMax = 3,       informationRates = c(179, 266, 298)/298,       alpha = 0.025, typeAlphaSpending = "sfOF") #> [1] 0.3662944 0.5550158

text: INew maximum information active arm versus common control secondary trial. title: Power and Sample Size for a Generic Group Sequential Design — getDesign
text: beta type II error. IMax maximum information. Either beta IMax provided one missing. theta parameter value. Null hypothesis theta = 0, alternative hypothesis one-sided theta > 0. kMax maximum number stages. informationRates information rates. Fixed prior trial. Defaults (1:kMax) / kMax left unspecified. efficacyStopping Indicators whether efficacy stopping allowed stage. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. futilityStopping Indicators whether futility stopping allowed stage. Defaults TRUE left unspecified. criticalValues Upper boundaries z-test statistic scale stopping efficacy. alpha significance level. Defaults 0.025. typeAlphaSpending type alpha spending. One following: "" O'Brien-Fleming boundaries, "P" Pocock boundaries, "WT" Wang & Tsiatis boundaries, "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "user" user defined spending, "none" early efficacy stopping. Defaults "sfOF". parameterAlphaSpending parameter value alpha spending. Corresponds \(\Delta\) "WT", \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". userAlphaSpending user defined alpha spending. Cumulative alpha spent stage. futilityBounds Lower boundaries z-test statistic scale stopping futility stages 1, ..., kMax-1. Defaults rep(-8, kMax-1) left unspecified. futility bounds non-binding calculation critical values. futilityCP futility bounds conditional power scale. futilityTheta futility bounds parameter scale. typeBetaSpending type beta spending. One following: "sfOF" O'Brien-Fleming type spending function, "sfP" Pocock type spending function, "sfKD" Kim & DeMets spending function, "sfHSD" Hwang, Shi & DeCani spending function, "user" user defined spending, "none" early futility stopping. Defaults "none". parameterBetaSpending parameter value beta spending. Corresponds \(\rho\) "sfKD", \(\gamma\) "sfHSD". userBetaSpending user defined beta spending. Cumulative beta spent stage. spendingTime vector length kMax error spending time analysis. Defaults missing, case, informationRates. varianceRatio ratio variance H0 variance H1.

title: Power and Sample Size for a Generic Group Sequential Design — getDesign
text: S3 class design object three components: overallResults: data frame containing following variables: overallReject: overall rejection probability. alpha: overall significance level. attainedAlpha: attained significance level, different overall significance level presence futility stopping. kMax: number stages. theta: parameter value. information: maximum information. expectedInformationH1: expected information H1. expectedInformationH0: expected information H0. drift: drift parameter, equal theta*sqrt(information). inflationFactor: inflation factor (relative fixed design). byStageResults: data frame containing following variables: informationRates: information rates. efficacyBounds: efficacy boundaries Z-scale. futilityBounds: futility boundaries Z-scale. rejectPerStage: probability efficacy stopping. futilityPerStage: probability futility stopping. cumulativeRejection: cumulative probability efficacy stopping. cumulativeFutility: cumulative probability futility stopping. cumulativeAlphaSpent: cumulative alpha spent. efficacyTheta: efficacy boundaries parameter scale. futilityTheta: futility boundaries parameter scale. efficacyP: efficacy boundaries p-value scale. futilityP: futility boundaries p-value scale. information: cumulative information. efficacyStopping: Whether allow efficacy stopping. futilityStopping: Whether allow futility stopping. rejectPerStageH0: probability efficacy stopping H0. futilityPerStageH0: probability futility stopping H0. cumulativeRejectionH0: cumulative probability efficacy stopping H0. cumulativeFutilityH0: cumulative probability futility stopping H0. settings: list containing following input parameters: typeAlphaSpending: type alpha spending. parameterAlphaSpending: parameter value alpha spending. userAlphaSpending: user defined alpha spending. typeBetaSpending: type beta spending. parameterBetaSpending: parameter value beta spending. userBetaSpending: user defined beta spending. spendingTime: error spending time analysis. varianceRatio: ratio variance H0 variance H1.

title: Power and Sample Size for a Generic Group Sequential Design — getDesign
text: function determines efficacy futility bounds based inputs provided, following clear priority order. Efficacy bounds: criticalValues supplied, take precedence alpha-spending parameters ignored. Otherwise, efficacy bounds derived specified alpha-spending function. Futility bounds: Futility inputs evaluated following order priority: futilityBounds provided, override futility-related inputs (futilityCP, futilityTheta, beta-spending parameters). futilityBounds provided futilityCP specified, futilityTheta beta-spending parameters ignored. futilityTheta provided, beta-spending parameters ignored. none futilityBounds, futilityCP, futilityTheta specified, futility bounds computed using beta-spending approach.

text: beta type II error. n total sample size. ngroups number treatment groups. means treatment group means. stDev common standard deviation. allocationRatioPlanned Allocation ratio treatment groups. length ngroups - 1 ngroups. length ngroups - 1, last treatment group assume value 1 allocation ratio. rounding Whether round sample size. title: Power and Sample Size for One-Way ANOVA — getDesignANOVA
text: S3 class designANOVA object following components: power: power reject null hypothesis difference among treatment groups. alpha: two-sided significance level. n: number subjects. ngroups: number treatment groups. means: treatment group means. stDev: common standard deviation. effectsize: effect size. allocationRatioPlanned: Allocation ratio treatment groups. rounding: Whether round sample size.

title: Power and Sample Size for One-Way ANOVA — getDesignANOVA
text: Let \(\{\mu_i: =1,\ldots,k\}\) denote group means, \(\{r_i: =1,\ldots,k\}\) denote randomization probabilities \(k\) treatment groups. Let \(\sigma\) denote common standard deviation, \(n\) denote total sample size. \(F\)-statistic $$F = \frac{SSR/(k-1)}{SSE/(n-k)} \sim F_{k-1, n-k, \lambda}$$ $$\lambda = n \sum_{=1}^k r_i (\mu_i - \bar{\mu})^2/\sigma^2$$ noncentrality parameter, \(\bar{\mu} = \sum_{=1}^k r_i \mu_i\).

title: Power and Sample Size for One-Way ANOVA — getDesignANOVA

text: S3 class designAgreement object following components: power: power reject null hypothesis. alpha: one-sided significance level. n: total sample size. ncats: number categories. kappaH0: kappa coefficient null hypothesis. kappa: kappa coefficient alternative hypothesis. p1: marginal probabilities first rater. p2: marginal probabilities second rater. piH0: cell probabilities maximize variance estimated kappa H0. pi: cell probabilities maximize variance estimated kappa H1. rounding: Whether round sample size.

title: Power and Sample Size for Cohen's kappa — getDesignAgreement
text: kappa coefficient defined $$\kappa = \frac{\pi_o - \pi_e}{1 - \pi_e},$$ \(\pi_o = \sum_i \pi_{ii}\) observed agreement, \(\pi_e = \sum_i \pi_{.} \pi_{.}\) expected agreement chance. Fleiss et al. (1969), variance \(\hat{\kappa}\) given $$Var(\hat{\kappa}) = \frac{v_1}{n},$$ $$v_1 = \frac{Q_1 + Q_2 - Q3 - Q4}{(1-\pi_e)^4},$$ $$Q_1 = \pi_o(1-\pi_e)^2,$$ $$Q_2 = (1-\pi_o)^2 \sum_i \sum_j \pi_{ij}(\pi_{.} + \pi_{.j})^2,$$ $$Q_3 = 2(1-\pi_o)(1-\pi_e) \sum_i \pi_{ii}(\pi_{.} + \pi_{.}),$$ $$Q_4 = (\pi_o \pi_e - 2\pi_e + \pi_o)^2.$$ Given \(\kappa\) marginals \(\{(\pi_{.}, \pi_{.}): =1,\ldots,k\}\), obtain \(\pi_o\). unknowns double summation \(Q_2\) single summation \(Q_3\). find optimal configuration cell probabilities yield maximum variance \(\hat{\kappa}\) treating problem linear programming problem constraints match given marginal probabilities observed agreement ensure cell probabilities nonnegative. extension Flack et al. (1988) allowing unequal marginal probabilities two raters. perform optimization null alternative hypotheses obtain \(\max Var(\hat{\kappa} | \kappa = \kappa_0)\) \(\max Var(\hat{\kappa} | \kappa = \kappa_1)\) single subject, calculate sample size power according following equation: $$\sqrt{n} |\kappa - \kappa_0| = z_{1-\alpha} \sqrt{\max Var(\hat{\kappa} | \kappa = \kappa_0)} + z_{1-\beta} \sqrt{\max Var(\hat{\kappa} | \kappa = \kappa_1)}.$$

title: Power and Sample Size for a Generic Group Sequential Equivalence Design — getDesignEquiv
text: S3 class designEquiv object three components: overallResults: data frame containing following variables: overallReject: overall rejection probability. alpha: overall significance level. attainedAlphaH10: attained significance level H10. attainedAlphaH20: attained significance level H20. kMax: number stages. thetaLower: parameter value lower equivalence limit. thetaUpper: parameter value upper equivalence limit. theta: parameter value alternative hypothesis. information: maximum information. expectedInformationH1: expected information H1. expectedInformationH10: expected information H10. expectedInformationH20: expected information H20. byStageResults: data frame containing following variables: informationRates: information rates. efficacyBounds: efficacy boundaries Z-scale two one-sided tests. rejectPerStage: probability efficacy stopping. cumulativeRejection: cumulative probability efficacy stopping. cumulativeAlphaSpent: cumulative alpha two one-sided tests. cumulativeAttainedAlphaH10: cumulative probability efficacy stopping H10. cumulativeAttainedAlphaH20: cumulative probability efficacy stopping H20. efficacyThetaLower: efficacy boundaries parameter scale one-sided null hypothesis lower equivalence limit. efficacyThetaUpper: efficacy boundaries parameter scale one-sided null hypothesis upper equivalence limit. efficacyP: efficacy bounds p-value scale two one-sided tests. information: cumulative information. settings: list containing following components: typeAlphaSpending: type alpha spending. parameterAlphaSpending: parameter value alpha spending. userAlphaSpending: user defined alpha spending. spendingTime: error spending time analysis.

title: Power and Sample Size for a Generic Group Sequential Equivalence Design — getDesignEquiv
text: Consider equivalence design two one-sided hypotheses: $$H_{10}: \theta \leq \theta_{10},$$ $$H_{20}: \theta \geq \theta_{20}.$$ reject \(H_{10}\) look \(k\) $$Z_{1j} = (\hat{\theta}_j - \theta_{10})\sqrt{I_j} \geq b_j$$ \(j=1,\ldots,k\), \(\{b_j:j=1,\ldots,K\}\) critical values associated specified alpha-spending function, \(I_j\) information \(\theta\) (inverse variance \(\hat{\theta}\)) \(j\)th look. example, estimating risk difference \(\theta = \pi_1 - \pi_2\), $$I_j = \left\{\frac{\pi_1 (1-\pi_1)}{n_{1j}} + \frac{\pi_2(1-\pi_2)}{n_{2j}}\right\}^{-1}.$$ follows $$(Z_{1j} \geq b_j) = (Z_j \geq b_j + \theta_{10}\sqrt{I_j}),$$ \(Z_j = \hat{\theta}_j \sqrt{I_j}\). Similarly, reject \(H_{20}\) look \(k\) $$Z_{2j} = (\hat{\theta}_j - \theta_{20})\sqrt{I_j} \leq -b_j$$ \(j=1,\ldots,k\). $$(Z_{2j} \leq -b_j) = (Z_j \leq - b_j + \theta_{20}\sqrt{I_j}).$$ Let \(l_j = b_j + \theta_{10}\sqrt{I_j}\), \(u_j = -b_j + \theta_{20}\sqrt{I_j}\). cumulative probability reject \(H_0 = H_{10} \cup H_{20}\) look \(k\) alternative hypothesis \(H_1\) given $$P_\theta\left(\cup_{j=1}^{k} (Z_{1j} \geq b_j) \cap \cup_{j=1}^{k} (Z_{2j} \leq -b_j)\right) = p_1 + p_2 - p_{12},$$ $$p_1 = P_\theta\left(\cup_{j=1}^{k} (Z_{1j} \geq b_j)\right) = P_\theta\left(\cup_{j=1}^{k} (Z_j \geq l_j)\right),$$ $$p_2 = P_\theta\left(\cup_{j=1}^{k} (Z_{2j} \leq -b_j)\right) = P_\theta\left(\cup_{j=1}^{k} (Z_j \leq u_j)\right),$$ $$p_{12} = P_\theta\left(\cup_{j=1}^{k} (Z_j \geq l_j) \cup (Z_j \leq u_j)\right).$$ note, \(p_1\) \(p_2\) can evaluated using one-sided exit probabilities group sequential designs. exists \(j\leq k\) \(l_j \leq u_j\), \(p_{12} = 1\). Otherwise, \(p_{12}\) can evaluated using two-sided exit probabilities group sequential designs. Since equivalent hypothesis tested using two one-sided tests, type error controlled. evaluate attained type error equivalence trial \(H_{10}\) (\(H_{20}\)), simply fix control group parameters, update active treatment group parameters according null hypothesis, use parameters power calculation outlined .

text: S3 class designLogistic object following components: power: power reject null hypothesis. alpha: two-sided significance level. n: total sample size. ncovariates: number covariates. nconfigs: number configurations discretized covariate values. x: matrix covariate values. pconfigs: vector probabilities configurations. corr: multiple correlation predictor covariates. oddsratios: odds ratios one unit increase covariates. responseprob: response probability full model predictor variables equal means. effectsize: effect size chi-square test. rounding: Whether round sample size.

title: Power and Sample Size for Logistic Regression — getDesignLogistic
text: consider logistic regression binary response variable \(Y\) set predictor variables \(x = (x_1,\ldots,x_K)^T\) \(x_1\) covariate interest: \(\log \frac{P(Y_i=1)}{1 - P(Y_i = 1)} = \psi_0 + x_i^T \psi,\) \(\psi = (\psi_1,\ldots,\psi_K)^T\). Similar Self et al (1992), assume covariates either inherently discrete discretized continuous distributions (e.g. using quantiles). Let \(m\) denote total number configurations covariate values. Let $$\pi_i = P(x = x_i), = 1,\ldots, m$$ denote probabilities configurations covariates independence. likelihood ratio test statistic testing \(H_0: \psi_1 = 0\) can approximated noncentral chi-square distribution one degree freedom noncentrality parameter $$\Delta = 2 \sum_{=1}^m \pi_i [b'(\theta_i)(\theta_i - \theta_i^*) - \{b(\theta_i) - b(\theta_i^*)\}],$$ $$\theta_i = \psi_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{k} \psi_j x_{ij},$$ $$\theta_i^* = \psi_0^* + \sum_{j=2}^{k} \psi_j^* x_{ij},$$ \(\psi_0^* = \psi_0 + \psi_1 \mu_1\), \(\psi_j^* = \psi_j\) \(j=2,\ldots,K\). \(\mu_1\) mean \(x_1\), e.g., \(\mu_1 = \sum_i \pi_i x_{i1}.\) addition, formulating logistic regression framework generalized linear models, $$b(\theta) = \log(1 + \exp(\theta)),$$ $$b'(\theta) = \frac{\exp(\theta)}{1 + \exp(\theta)}.$$ regression coefficients \(\psi\) can obtained taking log odds ratios covariates. intercept \(\psi_0\) can derived $$\psi_0 = \log(\bar{\mu}/(1- \bar{\mu})) - \sum_{j=1}^{K} \psi_j \mu_j,$$ \(\bar{\mu}\) denotes response probability predictor variables equal means. Finally, let \(\rho\) denote multiple correlation predictor covariates. noncentrality parameter chi-square test adjusted downward multiplying \(1-\rho^2\).

title: Power and Sample Size for Logistic Regression — getDesignLogistic
text: Steven G. Self, Robert H. Mauritsen Jill Ohara. Power calculations likelihood ratio tests generalized linear models. Biometrics 1992; 48:31-39.

title: Power and Sample Size for Logistic Regression — getDesignLogistic
code: # two ordinal covariates x1 = c(5, 10, 15, 20) px1 = c(0.2, 0.3, 0.3, 0.2)  x2 = c(2, 4, 6) px2 = c(0.4, 0.4, 0.2)  # discretizing a normal distribution with mean 4 and standard deviation 2 nbins = 10 x3 = qnorm(((1:nbins) - 0.5)/nbins)*2 + 4 px3 = rep(1/nbins, nbins)  # combination of covariate values nconfigs = length(x1)*length(x2)*length(x3) x = expand.grid(x3 = x3, x2 = x2, x1 = x1) x = as.matrix(x[, ncol(x):1])  # probabilities for the covariate configurations under independence pconfigs = as.numeric(px1 %x% px2 %x% px3)  # convert the odds ratio for the predictor variable in 5-unit change # to the odds ratio in 1-unit change (design1 <- getDesignLogistic(   beta = 0.1, ncovariates = 3,   nconfigs = nconfigs,   x = x,   pconfigs = pconfigs,   oddsratios = c(1.2^(1/5), 1.4, 1.3),   responseprob = 0.25,   alpha = 0.1)) #>   alpha     power    n ncovariates corr responseprob oddsratio  effectsize #> 1   0.1 0.9001583 1369           3    0         0.25  1.037137 0.006259349

title: Group Sequential Design for Two-Sample Mean Difference From the MMRM Model — getDesignMeanDiffMMRM
text: Consider longitudinal study two treatment groups. outcome measured baseline \(k\) postbaseline time points. treatment group, outcomes assumed follow multivariate normal distribution. Conditional baseline, covariance matrix post-baseline outcomes denoted \(\Sigma_1\) active treatment group \(\Sigma_2\) control group. Let \(\mu_1\) \(\mu_2\) denote mean vectors post-baseline outcomes active control groups, respectively. interested testing null hypothesis \(H_0: \mu_{1,k} - \mu_{2,k} = \delta_0\) alternative \(H_1: \mu_{1,k} - \mu_{2,k} = \delta\). study design based information treatment difference last postbaseline time point. information given $$= 1/\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}_{1,k} - \hat{\mu}_{2,k})$$ presence monotone missing data, let \(p_{g,1},\ldots,p_{g,k}\) denote proportions subjects observed data patterns 1 \(k\) treatment group \(g=1\) (active) 2 (control). subject pattern \(j\) complete data time \(t_j\), .e., observed outcomes \(y_{,1},\ldots,y_{,j}\), missing values \(y_{,j+1},\ldots,y_{,k}\). According Lu et al. (2008), information matrix post-baseline mean vector group \(g\) $$I_g = n \pi_g J_g$$ \(\pi_g\) proportion subjects group \(g\), $$J_g = \sum_{j=1}^k p_{g,j} \left( \begin{array}{cc} \Sigma_{g,j}^{-1} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right)$$ , \(\Sigma_{g,j}\) leading \(j\times j\) principal submatrix \(\Sigma_g\). follows $$\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}_{1,k} - \hat{\mu}_{2,k}) = \frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{1}{\pi_1} J_1^{-1}[k,k] + \frac{1}{\pi_2} J_2^{-1}[k,k]\right)$$ observed data pattern probabilities depend accrual dropout distributions. Let \(H(u)\) denote cumulative distribution function enrollment time \(u\), \(G_g(t)\) denote survival function dropout time \(t\) treatment group \(g\), \(\tau\) denote calendar time interim final analysis. , \(j=1,\ldots,k-1\), probability subject group \(g\) falls observed data pattern \(j\) $$p_{g,j} = H(\tau - t_j)G_g(t_j) - H(\tau - t_{j+1})G_g(t_{j+1})$$ last pattern (\(j=k\), .e., completers), $$p_{g,k} = H(\tau - t_k)G_g(t_k)$$ final analysis, \(\tau\) study duration, \(H(\tau - t_j) = 1\) \(j\). Therefore, pattern probabilities depend dropout distribution: $$p_{g,j} = G_g(t_j) - G_g(t_{j+1}), \quad j=1,\ldots,k-1$$ $$p_{g,k} = G_g(t_k)$$ Cumulative dropout probabilities post-baseline time points can used define piecewise exponential dropout distribution. Let \(F_g(t_j)\) denote cumulative probability dropout time \(t_j\) treatment group \(g\). left endpoints piecewise survival time intervals given \(t_0=0,t_1,\ldots,t_{k-1}\). hazard rate interval \((t_{j-1},t_j]\) given $$\gamma_{g,j} = -\log\left(\frac{1 - F_g(t_j)}{1 - F_g(t_{j-1})} \right) / (t_j - t_{j-1}), \quad j=1,\ldots,k$$

title: Group Sequential Design for Two-Sample Odds Ratio — getDesignOddsRatio
text: Consider group sequential design two-sample odds ratio. parameter interest $$\psi = \frac{\pi_1(1-\pi_2)}{(1-\pi_1)\pi_2}$$ \(\pi_1\) response probability active treatment group \(\pi_2\) response probability control group. statistical inference, parameter often transformed log scale: $$\theta = \log(\psi) = \log(\pi_1/(1-\pi_1)) - \log(\pi_2/(1-\pi_2))$$ variance estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) can derived binomial distributions follows: $$Var(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{1}{n} \{ \frac{1}{\pi_1(1-\pi_1) r} + \frac{1}{\pi_2(1-\pi_2)(1-r)} \}$$ \(n\) total number subjects \(r\) randomization probability active treatment group. nullVariance = TRUE, variance computed null hypothesis. case, values \(\pi_1\) \(\pi_2\) variance formula replaced restricted maximum likelihood counterparts, subject constraint $$\frac{\pi_1(1-\pi_2)}{(1-\pi_1)\pi_2}= \psi_0$$

text: S3 class designOneMultinom object following components: power: power reject null hypothesis. alpha: two-sided significance level. n: maximum number subjects. ncats: number categories multinomial response. piH0: prevalence category null hypothesis. pi: prevalence category. effectsize: effect size chi-square test. rounding: Whether round sample size.

title: Power and Sample Size for One-Sample Multinomial Response — getDesignOneMultinom
text: single-arm multinomial response design used test whether prevalence category different null hypothesis prevalence. null hypothesis prevalence category equal \(\pi_{0i}\), alternative hypothesis prevalence category equal \(\pi_i\), \(=1,\ldots,C\), \(C\) number categories. sample size calculated based chi-square test multinomial response. test statistic given $$X^2 = \sum_{=1}^{C} \frac{(n_i - n\pi_{0i})^2}{n\pi_{0i}}$$ \(n_i\) number subjects category \(\), \(n\) total sample size. null hypothesis, \(X^2\) follows chi-square distribution \(C-1\) degrees freedom. alternative hypothesis, \(X^2\) follows non-central chi-square distribution non-centrality parameter $$\lambda = n \sum_{=1}^{C} \frac{(\pi_i - \pi_{0i})^2}{\pi_{0i}}$$ sample size chosen power reject null hypothesis least \(1-\beta\) given significance level \(\alpha\).

title: Group Sequential Design for One-Sample Slope — getDesignOneSlope
text: assume simple linear regression form $$y_i = \alpha + \beta x_i + \epsilon_i$$ \(\epsilon_i\) residual error, assumed normally distributed mean 0 standard deviation \(\sigma_\epsilon\). covariate \(x_i\) assumed normally distributed mean 0 standard deviation \(\sigma_x\). slope null hypothesis \(\beta_0\), slope alternative hypothesis \(\beta\). Since $$\hat{\beta} = \frac{\sum_{=1}^{n} (x_i-\bar{x}) y_i} {\sum_{=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})^2}$$ follows $$\hat{\beta} \sim N(\beta,  \frac{\sigma_\epsilon^2}{\sum_{=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})^2}).$$ Since variance \(\hat{\beta}\) $$\frac{\sigma_\epsilon^2}{n\sigma_x^2}$$ can use calculate power sample size group sequential design.
